talk_metadata.talk_title: Five‑year update on implementation of Project Optimus
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "for all four of these cases, actually, the lower dose or the dosing regimen with the lower doses became the recommended dosage."

Speaker said: "there are continued challenges for dosage optimization, and these include making sure that the dosage optimization approach is appropriate to the specific drug and program rather than something that's a one size fits all."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of early dose optimization leading to high‑dose toxicities that later reduced overall survival

key_data_gaps: No randomized controlled trial data presented for the highlighted lung cancer agents
No OS data shown for the case examples

crispro_opportunity.description: Project Optimus case examples show dose selection without biomarker‑driven stratification; CrisPRO could provide predictive biomarkers to guide dose selection earlier.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that "the lower dose ... became the recommended dosage" without mentioning biomarker selection.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO's computational modeling to integrate genomic and pharmacodynamic biomarkers for dose‑response prediction.
external_validation_needed: ClinicalTrials.gov: retrieve trial designs for the four lung cancer agents
PubMed: biomarker studies linked to dose optimization of ADCs, bispecifics, and kinase inhibitors

cited_competitors.name: PI3K kinase inhibitors
cited_competitors.context: cited as examples where lack of dose optimization led to detrimental overall survival

assets_to_track: Bispecific T‑cell engager with dose optimization
companies_to_monitor: Companies developing the four highlighted lung cancer agents

talk_metadata.talk_title: Utilizing randomization, backfill and expansion cohorts to gain greater understanding in phase‑one trials
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We implemented it in an institutional database so that Bayesian adaptive model‑based design can happen behind the scenes, and we get predictions so that the patient can get assigned to the right dose at the time of patient registration."
Speaker said: "Randomization is here to balance the unknown and unmeasured factors, and this is why we still have risk factors that are unknown or not quantified at baseline."

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Phase‑1 dose‑finding for heterogeneous tumor subgroups
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Enrolling multiple disease histologies and dose levels without prospective biomarker stratification, risking dilution of signal across subgroups
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Molecular or histology‑specific predictive biomarker to enrich each dose cohort
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Small, non‑powered cohorts may not allow detection of dose‑response differences across subgroups

key_data_gaps: No quantitative data on how many patients per subgroup were needed to achieve statistical confidence

crispro_opportunity.description: Speaker advocates Bayesian adaptive designs; CrisPRO can augment these designs with AI‑driven exposure‑response modeling to improve dose selection efficiency.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described Bayesian adaptive model‑based designs and randomization to balance unknown factors.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Integrate CrisPRO's computational spine to generate real‑time dose‑allocation probabilities within the Bayesian framework.
external_validation_needed: PubMed: validation studies of Bayesian adaptive designs in oncology phase‑1 trials
ClinicalTrials.gov: identify trials using backfill or expansion cohorts

unresolved_questions: How many dose levels and subgroups are optimal before the trial becomes underpowered?

assets_to_track: Bayesian adaptive phase‑1 platform at MSK

talk_metadata.talk_title: Ensuring tolerability by incorporating patient‑reported outcomes in dose‑finding clinical trials
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "The patient may be feeling not so great... the clinician may think that's a mild toxicity and not really a cause for concern, and they write down a Grade 1 or Grade 2 toxicity."

Speaker said: "There is a lack of consistency on the instruments that are being used, either a quality of life instrument sometimes, and sometimes they're most based on symptoms, which they're different in nature."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Reliance on CTCAE Grade 3+ events ignores persistent Grade 2 toxicities that patients find intolerable

key_data_gaps: No empirical data presented on how PRO thresholds affect dose‑selection outcomes

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate PRO‑CTCAE data as a patient‑centric biomarker to refine tolerability thresholds during dose escalation.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that "the PRO CTCAE has been used for collection of symptoms in dose‑finding clinical trials" and that current DLT definitions miss Grade 2 toxicities.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO to model combined clinician‑reported and patient‑reported toxicity scores to predict optimal biologic dose.
external_validation_needed: PubMed: studies linking PRO‑CTCAE scores to dose‑limiting toxicity outcomes
ClinicalTrials.gov: identify phase‑1 trials that have prospectively integrated PRO data

unresolved_questions: What quantitative PRO threshold should be set to define unacceptable tolerability in early‑phase trials?
